title: Motivational Interviewing for Addiction
subtitle: Meeting patients where they are in process of change
author: Ned

seo.title: Motivational Interviewing for Addiction | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Motivational Interviewing for Addiction. Evidence-based information on addiction and mental health from Plan Your Recovery, St. Louis.

body:
Even if you’re not a mental-health therapist, you’ve probably heard of the importance of open-ended questions. Open-ended questions are embraced and encouraged across an array of professions outside of counseling, including sales, nursing, and research. But why are open-ended questions beneficial, and what do they do?

In a nutshell, open-ended questions can prompt internal reflection without felt judgement or defensiveness. Open-ended questions are about understanding and empathizing, not prescribing change.

Open ended questions, along with affirmation, reflective listening, and summarizing, are the core skills used in an addiction treatment approach called Motivational Interviewing. Motivational interviewing is a powerful, compassion-based tool that helps clients find the motivation necessary to change unhealthy behaviors. While motivational interviewing is often used by substance use professionals, it is also used by doctors, nurses, and other healthcare professionals. It’s one of the most robustly studied interventions available for addiction treatment, and can help clients feel engaged and heard during the treatment process.

Motivational interviewing is all about evoking a client’s change talk (eg: why I should stop using substances), instead of sustain talk (eg: the reasons I enjoy using substances). Somewhat counterintuitively, this is done by rolling with resistance (eg: “It sounds like cocaine use has been an important part of your life”), rather than fighting it (eg: “Why don’t you stop using cocaine? You’re ruining everything!”). The word “should” is the enemy of the motivational interviewer; instead of aggressively pushing a client towards change, the motivational interviewer walks beside the client, gently helping them weigh their options to find the best solution. 

Because a good motivational interviewer is a facilitator, not a prohibitor or antagonist, you should never hear a motivational interviewer argue with a client. The theory behind motivational interviewing recognizes that to evoke change, conversational space must also be given to the reasons a client may not want to not change a behavior. Expressing both sides of ambivalence brings the reality of the situation into the open, permitting the therapist to understand what holds a client back from change. MI is ideal for the client who isn’t sure if they’re ready for change, but is okay with talking about the pro’s and con’s of their current behavior.

At Plan Your Recovery, Motivational Interviewing is one of our foundational approaches to addiction. We understand that not every client is ready for big changes the moment they walk through our door, and that recovery happens with many small steps toward a larger goal. Regardless of where you are on that journey, we’re here to walk with you and help you navigate the terrain.
